questions.question_text: A seller is selling a 6-unit apartment building in California. Is a Transfer Disclosure Statement required?
questions.correct_answer: No, TDS applies only to 1-4 unit residential properties
distractors: Yes, TDS is required for all residential sales
Yes, but only for buildings built before 1978
No, TDS is optional for properties with more than 4 units
questions.explanation: The TDS is mandatory only for sales of 1-4 unit residential properties. A 6-unit building exceeds that threshold and is therefore exempt from the TDS requirement.

questions.question_text: A seller of a single-family home completes Section 1 of the TDS. The listing agent completes Section 2 after a visual inspection. The selling agent has not yet conducted an inspection. Is the TDS complete?
questions.correct_answer: No, all three sections must be completed including the selling agent's inspection
distractors: Yes, the seller and listing agent sections are sufficient
Yes, if the buyer waives the selling agent's section in writing
No, but the selling agent may rely on the listing agent's findings
questions.explanation: The TDS has three mandatory sections: (1) seller's declaration, (2) listing agent's inspection, and (3) selling agent's independent inspection. All three must be completed before the TDS is considered complete.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the seller required to disclose on the TDS?
questions.correct_answer: All material facts known to the seller about the property
distractors: Only defects visible during the agent's walkthrough
Only defects that have required past repairs
Defects that will cost more than $500 to fix
questions.explanation: The seller's TDS declaration requires disclosure of all known material facts about the property's condition, regardless of cost, visibility, or repair history.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs an offer on a home and receives the TDS two days later by mail. How many days does the buyer have to rescind the contract?
questions.correct_answer: 5 days from the date the TDS was mailed
distractors: 3 days from the date of signing the offer
3 days from receipt of the TDS
No right to rescind once the offer was signed
questions.explanation: When the TDS is delivered by mail, the buyer has 5 days (not 3) from the date of mailing to rescind. If the TDS had been delivered in person, the rescission period would be 3 days.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives the TDS after signing the purchase agreement but before close of escrow. The TDS reveals significant undisclosed plumbing issues. The buyer wants to back out. The buyer may:
questions.correct_answer: Rescind within 3 days of in-person delivery even after signing the offer
distractors: Rescind only if the TDS was not provided before the offer was accepted
Not rescind because the offer has already been accepted
Rescind only with seller's written consent
questions.explanation: California gives buyers a rescission right even after the offer is accepted, as long as the TDS was not delivered before the offer. The buyer has 3 days from in-person delivery (5 days if mailed) to rescind without penalty.

questions.question_text: A home is being sold through a court-ordered probate proceeding. Is a TDS required?
questions.correct_answer: No, court-ordered sales such as probate are exempt from TDS
distractors: Yes, TDS is always required for residential sales
Yes, but the probate attorney may complete it on behalf of the estate
No, only if the deceased owner had lived in the property fewer than 2 years
questions.explanation: California exempts several categories of sales from TDS requirements, including court-ordered sales such as probate, foreclosure, and bankruptcy proceedings.

questions.question_text: A bank is selling an REO (bank-owned) property. The bank's attorney claims TDS is not required. Is this correct?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, REO sales are among the exemptions from TDS requirements
distractors: No, REO sales require TDS like any other residential sale
No, but the bank may use an 'as-is' addendum instead of a TDS
Yes, but only if the bank never occupied the property
questions.explanation: REO (real estate owned) properties — homes acquired by lenders through foreclosure — are exempt from the TDS requirement. This is consistent with the broader exemption for court-ordered sales.

questions.question_text: A person who committed suicide in a home died 2.5 years before the sale. Must the seller disclose this on the TDS?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, deaths within the past 3 years must be disclosed
distractors: No, suicides are not material facts under California law
Yes, but only if the buyer asks directly about prior deaths
No, only homicides require disclosure
questions.explanation: California requires sellers to disclose deaths that occurred on the property within the past 3 years, regardless of cause. A suicide 2.5 years ago falls within this window and must be disclosed.

questions.question_text: A seller knows a murder occurred on the property 4 years ago. A buyer asks point-blank: 'Has anyone ever died in this house?' How must the seller respond?
questions.correct_answer: The seller must truthfully disclose the death when directly asked
distractors: The seller may decline to answer since the death was over 3 years ago
The seller may say no because the disclosure period has passed
The seller must disclose only if the death was widely publicized
questions.explanation: While sellers have no obligation to proactively disclose deaths more than 3 years old, they may never lie when directly asked. If a buyer asks, the seller must answer truthfully.

questions.question_text: A California purchase contract must include a notice about which of the following?
questions.correct_answer: The Megan's Law sex offender database
distractors: The California Megadrought Water Conservation database
The California Criminal History database
The California Environmental Hazards Registry
questions.explanation: California law requires all residential purchase contracts to include a Megan's Law notice informing buyers that they may obtain information about registered sex offenders from law enforcement agencies.

questions.question_text: A buyer learns that a neighbor was arrested for drug manufacturing near the property two years ago. The buyer claims the property is 'psychologically affected' and wants to rescind. Under California law:
questions.correct_answer: The seller has no duty to disclose most psychologically affecting facts beyond the 3-year death rule
distractors: The buyer may rescind if a crime occurred within 1,000 feet of the property
The seller must disclose any criminal activity that reduced neighborhood values
The buyer has a statutory right to rescind for any stigma-related concern
questions.explanation: California limits the stigmatized property disclosure duty primarily to the 3-year death rule. Sellers are generally not required to disclose other psychologically affecting facts such as nearby criminal activity.

questions.question_text: A husband and wife co-own a property. The wife wants to transfer her interest to her husband at no cost. Is a TDS required?
questions.correct_answer: No, transfers between co-owners or spouses are exempt
distractors: Yes, all residential transfers require a TDS
Yes, because the transfer changes ownership interests
No, but only if the transfer is recorded with the county
questions.explanation: California exempts transfers between co-owners and between spouses from the TDS requirement. These intra-family or co-owner transfers are treated as non-arm's-length transactions not requiring the same level of disclosure.

questions.question_text: A new home builder selling newly constructed homes directly to buyers — is a TDS required?
questions.correct_answer: No, new construction is exempt from the TDS requirement
distractors: Yes, all residential sales require a TDS
Yes, but only if the builder has previously occupied the home
No, but the builder must provide a substitute disclosure form
questions.explanation: New construction sold directly by the builder is exempt from the TDS requirement. New homes have a different disclosure framework, including public reports from the DRE for subdivisions.

questions.question_text: The TDS is delivered to the buyer in person on the day the offer is accepted. The buyer decides five days later they want to rescind based on TDS disclosures. May the buyer rescind?
questions.correct_answer: No, the 3-day in-person rescission window has passed
distractors: Yes, because 5 days is within the allowable rescission window
Yes, if the disclosed defect is above a certain dollar threshold
No, because the TDS was provided before offer acceptance
questions.explanation: For in-person delivery, the buyer has only 3 days to rescind based on TDS content. Five days after delivery exceeds this window, so the rescission right has lapsed.

questions.question_text: Under California law, which section of the TDS is completed by the selling (buyer's) agent?
questions.correct_answer: Section 3: Selling agent's independent visual inspection
distractors: Section 1: Seller's declaration of known defects
Section 2: Listing agent's visual inspection findings
All sections; the agent is responsible for the entire TDS
questions.explanation: Section 3 of the TDS is completed by the selling (buyer's) agent after conducting an independent visual inspection of the property. This is separate from and in addition to the listing agent's Section 2 inspection.
